narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 59.9% chance of not passing. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of upper-level law might prove an uphill battle. Right behind that was your 1st decile ranking in Year 1, painting a picture of early struggles that set a tough foundation from the start. Your middle family income added another layer, suggesting fewer resources to buffer the intense demands of law school compared to peers with more financial cushions. Then there was your LSAT score of 35.5 and undergraduate GPA of 2.8—solid efforts, but ones that signaled to the model you'd need to stretch further than most to compete in the high-stakes bar prep arena. Your background as a white male pursuing full-time studies didn't shift the tide much either, as those traits aligned with patterns where others in similar spots have faltered. It wasn't one thing, but this combination that built a narrative of steady challenges ahead, one the model couldn't ignore when forecasting your odds. You're clearly driven, though—full-time commitment shows heart—and with targeted steps like bolstering those study habits or seeking extra academic support, you could rewrite this story. Law school isn't the only door, and your persistence will open others. Hang in there; this is just one chapter.
